caveats: a: BLS data cover water, sewage, and other systems rather than 221320 alone and include public employers of all sizes.
a: EPA smart-sewer cases show technical and capital benefits, not wage-weighted labor substitution across an entire treatment operator.
rho: EPA case studies may select larger or more successful utilities than the private LMM population.
rho: Compliance requirements establish implementation friction but do not measure AI adoption or realized savings.
e: Most U.S. wastewater infrastructure is publicly owned, while the frozen firm count is derived from private-firm data; the exact surviving private business models are not observed.
e: Facility ownership, contract operator, asset owner, and permittee can be different legal entities, complicating lens classification.
s5: No national denominator of eligible privately controlled wastewater operators and completed control transfers was found.
s5: Strategic environmental-service buyers may increase transaction frequency for contract operators even when treatment assets themselves do not transfer.
q: Capture varies sharply among investor-owned asset utilities, municipal contract operators, public-private partnerships, industrial treatment contracts, and special districts.
q: Avoided fines and deferred capital are economically valuable but are not necessarily retained labor benefit or distributable margin.
d5: BLS projects broad NAICS 221300 including water systems, not wastewater treatment alone.
d5: EPA's $630.1 billion need includes stormwater, nonpoint sources, and decentralized systems outside 221320 and measures capital requirements rather than service quantity.
o: This estimates persistence of an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not survival of a particular owner, contract, or facility.
o: Decentralized treatment and reuse can relocate responsibility without eliminating the need for accountable operation.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring tariff, assessment, contracted, availability, capacity, volumetric, or wholesale revenue for accountable operation of sewer collection and wastewater treatment infrastructure serving external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Municipal departments without a transferable operating business, captive industrial treatment systems, septic-tank and portable-toilet services, sewer cleaning and rodding contractors, non-sewer waste treatment facilities, water supply utilities, construction contractors, and software or equipment vendors that do not operate an in-scope system.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators of sewer systems and sewage treatment facilities that collect, convey, treat, and dispose of wastewater for external residential, commercial, industrial, municipal, institutional, or wholesale customers.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS reports 58,320 jobs in NAICS 221300, including 23.11% office and administrative support, 32.13% production, 12.88% installation and repair, 9.54% construction, 3.67% business and financial operations, and 1.65% engineering; EPA documents real-time monitoring, modeling, analytics, and AI/ML in wastewater systems. Transformation: assigned high exposure to repetitive office, monitoring, reporting, forecasting, and analytical tasks; partial exposure to process support and engineering; and low exposure to physical, laboratory, emergency, and accountable control work, net of current automation. Error direction: water-system inclusion may distort the occupation mix and existing controls can make gross exposure overstate incremental opportunity, while manual reporting and inspection at small facilities may leave more headroom.
caveats: BLS data cover water, sewage, and other systems rather than 221320 alone and include public employers of all sizes.
EPA smart-sewer cases show technical and capital benefits, not wage-weighted labor substitution across an entire treatment operator.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study of private wastewater operators, netting out existing SCADA and controls and separating collection, treatment, laboratory, maintenance, office, and compliance work, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist billing, customer service, reporting, sampling-data review, inflow and infiltration analysis, overflow prediction, process optimization, maintenance planning, scheduling, procurement, and compliance documentation. Plant operation, laboratory and field sampling, physical repair, construction, emergency response, and accountable effluent-control decisions remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects real output for water, sewage, and other systems from 15.6 to 16.3 over ten years, a 0.5% annual rate; EPA reports $630.1 billion of twenty-year clean-water infrastructure need, including $66.6 billion for secondary treatment, $83.6 billion for advanced treatment, $151.1 billion for conveyance repair and new systems, and $36.5 billion for combined-sewer overflow correction. Transformation: used roughly five years of broad real-output growth as the center, then widened for wastewater-specific connections, load, wet weather, treatment-stringency, conservation, and outsourcing changes. Error direction: capital need and out-of-scope categories can overstate operating quantity, while stricter effluent quality and climate-driven wet-weather management can increase constant-quality service even with flat sewage volume.
caveats: BLS projects broad NAICS 221300 including water systems, not wastewater treatment alone.
EPA's $630.1 billion need includes stormwater, nonpoint sources, and decentralized systems outside 221320 and measures capital requirements rather than service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year facility and municipal forecasts of billed connections, influent volume and load, industrial discharge, permit-treatment requirements, reuse output, and outsourced operating scope would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: Population, new connections, industrial activity, aging collection systems, stricter effluent requirements, wet-weather control, and water reuse support constant-quality service demand. Conservation, infiltration reduction, drought, industrial closure, municipal insourcing, and slower connection growth can limit treated volume, while compliance intensity can rise even with flat flows.

caveats: Most U.S. wastewater infrastructure is publicly owned, while the frozen firm count is derived from private-firm data; the exact surviving private business models are not observed.
Facility ownership, contract operator, asset owner, and permittee can be different legal entities, complicating lens classification.
e.change_evidence: A record-level match of Census firms to EPA facility ownership, NPDES permittees, municipal contracts, normalized EBITDA, external billing, contract duration, and legal transferability would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: Private wastewater utilities and contracted operating entities can provide recurring external service and fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by public or quasi-public facilities, captive industrial systems, nonprofit or special-purpose entities without transferable economics, construction or cleaning businesses misclassified as operators, firms outside the EBITDA band, and contracts too short or concentrated to support a durable transfer.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221320 ($528,838,706) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221320 ($2,392,909,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $392,538 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221320); class <5: 175 firms, avg revenue/firm $455,029 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $158,896 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,498,868 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $523,405 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,187,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,113,160 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,516,278 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,973,884 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,602,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,749,877 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,834,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $42,195,260 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 89

caveats: This estimates persistence of an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not survival of a particular owner, contract, or facility.
Decentralized treatment and reuse can relocate responsibility without eliminating the need for accountable operation.
o.change_evidence: Permit and contract evidence quantifying municipal insourcing, industrial self-treatment, decentralized substitution, fully remote operations, consolidation, and facility abandonment would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
o.rationale: Wastewater collection and treatment still require an accountable operator to monitor, convey, treat, sample, report, maintain, secure, and respond to overflows and process failures under a discharge permit. Automation and smart sewers improve decisions but do not eliminate responsible operation; displacement is limited to municipal insourcing, consolidation, customer pretreatment or self-treatment, decentralized systems, or facility closure.

caveats: Capture varies sharply among investor-owned asset utilities, municipal contract operators, public-private partnerships, industrial treatment contracts, and special districts.
Avoided fines and deferred capital are economically valuable but are not necessarily retained labor benefit or distributable margin.
q.change_evidence: Rate-case and operating-contract evidence on expense trackers, cost-plus terms, gainsharing, rebid frequency, fixed versus volumetric charges, performance penalties, and post-efficiency revenue requirements would materially refine retention.
q.rationale: Essential monopoly service, tariff lag, fixed capacity charges, and long operating contracts can preserve some implemented benefit. Over five years, rate cases, municipal rebids, cost-plus or open-book contracts, performance sharing, affordability constraints, public governance, and required service improvements can pass a large share of savings to customers or reinvest it in compliance.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: EPA reports that many wastewater utilities use real-time monitoring, modeling, analytics, and AI/ML and provides deployed cases with overflow and capital benefits; EPA also states that small public and private wastewater plants must comply with NPDES permit effluent limits. Transformation: converted demonstrated technical use into the implemented share of exposed tasks over five years, discounting for sensor coverage, integration, capital, skills, validation, cybersecurity, permit accountability, and small-facility economics. Error direction: showcase cases can overstate general realization, while falling sensor and software costs and compliance pressure could accelerate adoption beyond the central case.
caveats: EPA case studies may select larger or more successful utilities than the private LMM population.
Compliance requirements establish implementation friction but do not measure AI adoption or realized savings.
rho.change_evidence: Facility-level deployment studies reporting baseline staffing, implementation and sensor cost, model performance, operator overrides, hours avoided, overflow and energy effects, and adoption by system size would materially refine implementation.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful in administrative work, monitoring, overflow prediction, capacity optimization, maintenance planning, reporting, and decision support because smart-sewer deployments are already operating. Realization remains constrained by sensors and data coverage, integration cost, old assets, small-system skills, permit validation, cybersecurity, false alarms, procurement, and the need for human oversight and field response.

caveats: No national denominator of eligible privately controlled wastewater operators and completed control transfers was found.
Strategic environmental-service buyers may increase transaction frequency for contract operators even when treatment assets themselves do not transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year NPDES permit-transfer records, municipal contract assignments, utility dockets, and private transaction data matched to control, EBITDA, environmental liabilities, and post-close continuity would materially move the probability.
s5.rationale: Privately controlled systems and contract operators can transfer through strategic consolidation or owner succession, but municipal consent, permit transfer, contract assignment, environmental liability, lender approval, and public scrutiny can delay or prevent qualifying sales. Many special-purpose and public structures are not conventionally saleable.

principal_driver: Mandatory wastewater collection, treatment, and discharge compliance create durable recurring operator-required service.
principal_weakness: Public ownership, environmental liability, capital needs, permit and contract approvals, and limited private transferability constrain firm availability and value capture.
